title: Arizona vs Michigan — Final Four Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • Final Four • Indianapolis, IN
venue: Lucas Oil Stadium, Indianapolis, Indiana • TBS
extraInfo: Winner advances to the National Championship Game on Monday, April 6 vs. the winner of UConn/Illinois

stakes.callout: Michigan 91, Arizona 73. KenPom #1 demolished KenPom #2 in the most lopsided Final Four game since 2021. The "mirror match" between two #1 seeds, two conference champions, and two +650 scoring differentials became an 18-point Michigan runaway. Dusty May's greatest-turnaround-in-history project is now one win from a national championship. Tommy Lloyd's first Final Four in 25 years ended in a blowout. Michigan advances to face UConn on Monday night.

cards.label: For Arizona
cards.value: The first Final Four since 2001 — 25 years of waiting. Tommy Lloyd broke through after five consecutive Elite Eight losses across multiple coaching eras. Koa Peat, the West Regional MOP, has 70 points and 27 rebounds in four tournament games. The three freshmen (Peat, Burries, Kharchenkov) combined for 52 points against Purdue in the Elite Eight comeback. Lloyd signed a contract extension through 2031, ending the North Carolina speculation. This is the same city where Lute Olson won the 1997 national championship.

cards.label: For Michigan
cards.value: The greatest turnaround in college basketball history is two wins from a national title. From 8-24 (dead last in Big Ten) to 35-3 and the Final Four in two years. Yaxel Lendeborg — cut from his high school team in Puerto Rico, turned down $7-9 million from Kentucky — is the Midwest Regional MOP averaging 25.0 PPG on 61.4% FG in the tournament. Dusty May's locker room sign reads "April Habits" — a lesson from FAU's 2023 Final Four. May: "I promise you, I'll be a lot more long winded when we're celebrating at the parade after we win the championship."

cards.label: The Mirror Match
cards.value: May said it best: "We feel like we're built in a similar fashion to Arizona... When we watch them, we see ourselves." Both teams have elite defense and offense. Both are top-5 in KenPom on both ends. Both have a dominant 7-foot-plus center. Both have a conference Player of the Year. Both won their conference regular season and tournament. The difference? Arizona scores in the paint (5th nationally, 42+ PPG). Michigan scores everywhere — 90+ in every tournament game, 44.6% from three. Something has to give.

cards.label: Nine NBA Draft Picks on One Floor
cards.value: Between Arizona's Peat, Burries, Kharchenkov, and Krivas and Michigan's Lendeborg, Mara, Johnson, Cadeau, and McKenney, there are approximately nine potential NBA draft picks on the court. ESPN ranked all 20 Final Four starters — Arizona has four in the top 10, led by Burries (#4) and Peat (#5). Michigan's Lendeborg is #1 overall. This is the highest concentration of pro talent in a Final Four game in years.

styles.title: Elite Two-Way Machine, Paint Dominance, Freshman Firepower
bullets: Historically dominant on both ends: Arizona ranks top-5 in offensive efficiency and top-10 in defensive efficiency — KenPom #2 overall. They're 36-2, opened with 23 straight wins, and swept the Big 12 regular season (16-2) and tournament. Only Kansas (road) and Texas Tech beat them all season. Four Arizona starters rank in the top 14 among all 20 Final Four starters per ESPN.

Paint dominance is the identity: Arizona ranks 5th nationally in paint points (42+ per game) and leads the nation in free throw attempts. Against Arkansas in the Sweet 16: 60 paint points and 30 FT points — the most combined in a tournament game in 20 years. Against Purdue in the Elite Eight: 40 paint points on 46.6% shooting. Krivas (7-2) and Peat (6-8) attack the rim relentlessly, creating space for perimeter shooters.

Three freshmen carrying the load: Burries (15.9 PPG, 68.4% from three in the tournament), Peat (17.5 PPG on 54.2% in the tournament, West Regional MOP), and Kharchenkov (14.0 PPG, 6.5 RPG in the tournament) have been sensational. They combined for 52 points against Purdue in the Elite Eight comeback from 7 down at halftime. They've shown zero fear in March.

Second-half adjustments are elite: Against Purdue, Arizona trailed 38-31 at halftime and outscored them 48-26 in the second half. Purdue went 0-of-8 from three after halftime. Against Utah State, Arizona shot 29% in the first half but rallied to win by 12. Lloyd: "Stay steady. Let's work ourselves back in the game." This team doesn't panic.

Weakness — perimeter defense and free throws: Arizona's three-point defense has been exploited at times — Utah State hit 8 threes, and the SI scouting report flagged it as their biggest vulnerability. Free throw shooting (69% vs. Utah State, though 90.9% vs. Purdue) has been inconsistent. Peat's FT% (59.7%) remains a concern. Michigan shoots 40%+ from three since March 1 — if the Wolverines get hot from deep, Arizona's paint dominance may not matter.

styles.title: America's #1 Defense, Historic Scoring, Transfer Portal Masterpiece
bullets: The best defense in America: Michigan is KenPom #1 in adjusted defensive efficiency, anchored by Aday Mara's 7-3 frame and 100+ blocks (a new program record). Opponents shoot 38% at the rim and 28% from three when Mara defends on the perimeter. Michigan held Tennessee to 32.0% shooting and 19.2% from three in the Elite Eight. They allowed just 28 second-half points against Alabama — the #1 scoring team in America.

90+ in every tournament game: Michigan is averaging 95.3 PPG in the tournament, winning by an average of 22.5 points. They're the first Michigan team to score 90+ in four consecutive tournament games — something even the 1989 national champions didn't do. They've made at least 10 three-pointers in all four games (44.6% from deep). Five different starters have led the team in scoring across the four games.

Lendeborg is the best player in the tournament: The Big Ten Player of the Year is averaging 25.0 PPG, 8.3 RPG, 4.3 APG on 61.4% FG and 52.6% from three in the tournament — with just 2 turnovers across four games. Named Midwest Regional MOP. ESPN ranked him the #1 starter in the entire Final Four. May: "He still has no idea how good he is."

Bench depth is devastating: Michigan's bench outscored Alabama's 33-6 in the Sweet 16. Trey McKenney (Mr. Basketball of Michigan, freshman) and Roddy Gayle Jr. provide reliable scoring off the bench. Nimari Burnett, a sixth-year senior, scored double figures in 3 of 4 tournament games. Michigan goes 8-9 deep without a significant drop-off.

Weakness — turnovers and slow starts: Michigan shot 28% from three in its three losses this season. They trailed Alabama at halftime and allowed Howard 14 threes and 46 first-half points. Against Purdue-like teams that can hit early threes, Michigan can fall behind. Turnovers (11.7/game) are the biggest vulnerability — Arizona forces 11.6 per game. If Arizona's pressure creates live-ball turnovers, Michigan's fast-break identity gets used against them.

keys.title: The Battle of the 7-Footers: Krivas vs. Mara in the post.
keys.description: Motiejus Krivas (7-2, 260 lbs) vs. Aday Mara (7-3, Big Ten DPOY, 100+ blocks). This is the marquee individual matchup. Krivas had 12 rebounds and 3 assists in the Elite Eight against Purdue and grabbed 9 offensive rebounds against Utah State. Mara is a rim-protecting force — opponents shoot just 38% at the rim when he's there. If Krivas can establish deep post position and generate second chances, Arizona controls the tempo. If Mara locks down the paint and forces Arizona into jumpers, Michigan's defensive identity takes over. The winner of this matchup likely wins the game.

keys.title: Michigan's three-point shooting vs. Arizona's perimeter defense — the game's swing factor.
keys.description: Michigan is shooting 44.6% from three in the tournament (40%+ since March 1) with at least 10 made threes in every game. Arizona held Arkansas to 21.7% from three in the Sweet 16, and Purdue went 0-of-8 from deep in the second half. But Michigan isn't one shooter — Lendeborg (52.6%), McKenney (38.4%), Cadeau (40%), and Burnett (38.1%) all connect from deep. Michigan shot 28% from three in their three losses. If Arizona can replicate the Purdue second-half treatment and disrupt Michigan's perimeter game, the Wolverines' offense stalls. If Michigan hits threes early, Arizona's paint dominance won't be enough.

keys.title: Arizona's paint dominance vs. Michigan's elite interior defense.
keys.description: Arizona ranks 5th nationally in paint points (42+ PPG) and scored 60 paint points against Arkansas and 40 against Purdue. They lead the nation in free throw attempts. Michigan's defense ranks in the top 5 in two-point defense. Peat (17.5 PPG on 54.2%), Kharchenkov (14.0 PPG), and Krivas attack the rim relentlessly. But Mara changes everything — his 7-3 wingspan alters shots even when he doesn't block them. Arizona's answer in the Elite Eight was free throws (20-of-22). Can they get to the line against Michigan's disciplined defense?

keys.title: Arizona's second-half comebacks vs. Michigan's second-half dominance.
keys.description: Arizona outscored Purdue 48-26 in the second half after trailing by 7 at halftime. Michigan held Alabama to 28 second-half points and outscored Tennessee by 29 in the second half. Both teams are elite closers — but for different reasons. Arizona relies on in-game adjustments and grinding you down with physicality. Michigan locks in defensively and uses its bench depth to wear teams out. Dusty May: "It's going to be won in the 39th or 40th minute of this game." If it's close late, whose adjustments and whose depth will prove decisive?

keys.title: The freshmen test: Can Arizona's trio handle the biggest stage?
keys.description: Arizona's three freshmen — Burries, Peat, and Kharchenkov — have combined for 170+ points in four tournament games. They showed zero nerves in the Elite Eight comeback against Purdue (52 combined points). But the Final Four is different. Lucas Oil Stadium holds 70,000+. The national TV audience is massive. Michigan's Will Tschetter said: "The one thing that stands out to me about them is just the relentlessness for 40 minutes." May has been to a Final Four before (FAU, 2023). Lloyd hasn't. But Arizona's freshmen have shown a poise that belies their age — and Jaden Bradley, the senior Big 12 POY, steadies the ship.

prediction.summary: Final Score: Michigan 91, Arizona 73. What was billed as a coin-flip between KenPom #1 and #2 turned into an 18-point demolition. Michigan jumped to a 10-1 lead in three minutes, built a 26-10 advantage, and led 48-32 at halftime — Arizona's largest halftime deficit all season. The Wolverines shot 47.8% from the field and 44.4% from three (12-27) while holding Arizona to 36.6% shooting. Aday Mara was sensational with a career-high 26 points on 11-16 shooting, 9 rebounds, and 2 blocks. Michigan scored 90+ for the fifth straight tournament game — an all-time NCAA Tournament record. The Wolverines advance to face UConn in Monday's national championship game.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes — Michigan Seizes Control Immediately
phases.description: Michigan came out firing, jumping to a 10-1 lead in the first three minutes. Aday Mara set the tone early with dominant post play. Michigan extended the lead to 26-10 before Arizona could catch its breath. Dusty May's game plan — packing the paint with Mara's 7-3 frame and cutting off Arizona's scoring within 15 feet — worked to perfection from the opening tip.

phases.title: Mid-First Half — Lendeborg Injury, Arizona's Brief Rally
phases.description: Arizona fought back with a 13-2 run to close within 28-23 — their best stretch of the game. But Yaxel Lendeborg stepped on Motiejus Krivas's foot while driving, rolling his left ankle and spraining his MCL. He hit both free throws before exiting at the 8:51 mark. Roddy Gayle Jr. broke Arizona's momentum with a dunk at 6:22, igniting a 7-0 Michigan run. Mara scored seven consecutive points. Halftime: Michigan 48, Arizona 32. Arizona had just 2 assists and 9 turnovers in the half.

phases.title: Second Half Opens — Lendeborg Returns, Michigan Buries Arizona
phases.description: Lendeborg returned to start the second half with his knee wrapped — and drained two three-pointers in 80 seconds, extending the lead to 56-34. The crowd of 72,111 erupted. Jaden Bradley picked up his fourth foul just 94 seconds into the half, essentially ending Arizona's hopes. Trey McKenney caught fire off the bench, hitting four three-pointers. Michigan's lead swelled to 30 points. The "highly anticipated" matchup became a dunkfest-slash-highlight reel.

phases.title: Final Stretch — Championship Game Awaits
phases.description: With the game long decided, Michigan cleared its bench. The Wolverines finished with 9 dunks and 18 assists on 33 made field goals. Tommy Lloyd acknowledged: "No one's been able to do that to us all year." Dusty May's postgame was measured: "This game was very indicative of how this group has played throughout the season — unselfish basketball." Michigan improved to 36-3 and heads to Monday's title game against UConn.

projectedScore.rationale: Michigan's three-point shooting (12-27, 44.4%) and Mara's paint dominance (26 pts, 11-16 FG) created separation Arizona never could close. The Wildcats' freshmen — who'd been sensational all tournament — shot a combined 31% (13-42). Bradley's early foul trouble removed Arizona's steadiest hand. Lendeborg's gritty return after the MCL/ankle injury inspired Michigan, and the Wolverines' depth (McKenney 16 pts, Burnett 7 pts off the bench) overwhelmed a Wildcats team that ran out of answers.

players.name: Koa Peat
players.meta: F • 6-8 • Freshman • West Regional MOP • Double-double in defeat
players.bio:
His name means "strong warrior" in Hawaiian — and he fought hard even in a losing effort. The West Regional MOP had 16 points and 11 rebounds (5 offensive), but Michigan's defense held him to 6-of-18 shooting — Mara's 7-3 frame neutralized his rim attacks. Son of former NFL guard Todd Peat. First player in USA Basketball history to win four junior gold medals. Had 86 points and 38 rebounds across five tournament games. Postgame: "It just wasn't our day honestly." Projected lottery pick who will return for a sophomore campaign.
Read more →

players.name: Jaden Bradley
players.stats: 13 PTS • 4-6 FG • 5-6 FT • Fouled out in 25 min
players.meta: G • 6-3 • Senior • Big 12 POY • 4th foul 94 sec into 2nd half
players.bio: The ultimate redemption arc ended on the bench with foul trouble. Bradley — the Big 12 Player of the Year who went from Alabama castoff to Third-Team All-American — picked up his fourth foul just 94 seconds into the second half, removing Arizona's steadiest hand when the Wildcats most needed composure. Despite efficient 4-of-6 shooting and 13 points, his 25 minutes were the fewest in any tournament game. Lloyd: "I don't know if I've seen a guy be as consistently high-level in clutch moments as he's been for an entire season." His Arizona career ends at 36-3.

players.name: Brayden Burries
players.meta: G • 6-4 • Freshman • Big 12 Newcomer of Year • Off night
players.bio: The worst shooting night of the tournament for the projected top-10 pick. After shooting 68.4% from three through four games, Burries went 4-of-16 (2-5 from three) with three air balls. Michigan's switching defense and length disrupted his rhythm from the opening tip. ESPN had ranked him the #4 starter in the Final Four. Postgame: "Sometimes it's just not your day." The five-star from California who scored 44 to win the state championship will have three more years to chase this stage.

players.name: Tobe Awaka
players.stats: 8 PTS • 4-7 FG • 9 REB • 17 min off bench
players.meta: F • Junior • Nation's top rebounder by percentage
players.bio: The one bright spot off Arizona's bench. Awaka provided a team-high 9 rebounds in just 17 minutes and shot an efficient 4-of-7 from the field. The nation's leader in rebounding percentage gave Arizona its only consistent energy when the starters struggled. While Krivas (2-8 FG, 7 pts) was outclassed by Mara in the marquee center matchup, Awaka's hustle kept Arizona on the glass — the Wildcats actually outrebounded Michigan 44-40 despite the 18-point loss.

players.name: Yaxel Lendeborg
players.meta: G/F • Big Ten POY • Injured in 1st half, returned in 2nd • "I'm playing no matter what"
players.bio:
The most inspiring performance of the Final Four — even in limited minutes. Lendeborg stepped on Krivas's foot while driving in the first half, spraining his MCL and re-injuring the ankle from the Big Ten Tournament. He hit both free throws, then left at the 8:51 mark. He returned to start the second half with his knee wrapped and drained two three-pointers in 80 seconds to push the lead to 22. Finished with 11 points in just 14 minutes (3-3 from three). MRI came back "clean." On Monday's title game: "I'm playing no matter what... Unless I wake up and fall off my feet." His mom's motto: "No pain. No gain."
Read more →

players.name: Aday Mara
players.meta: C • 7-3 • Big Ten DPOY • Only 2nd player 7-3+ to score 20+ in Final Four
players.bio: The best game of his career on the biggest stage. Mara's 26 points on 11-of-16 shooting made him only the second player 7-3 or taller to score 20+ in a Final Four game (joining Zach Edey). He completely neutralized Arizona's paint-dominant attack — the identity that carried the Wildcats to a 36-2 record. The 7-3 Spaniard who transferred from UCLA added 9 rebounds, 3 assists, and 2 blocks. May: "Aday was sensational. He was at the rim, catching lobs. He was a force down low. His basketball IQ is off the charts." Mara: "I'm so grateful and so happy that I'm able to play with this group."

players.name: Elliot Cadeau
players.meta: G • Transfer from UNC • 3rd double-double • Survived allergic reaction scare
players.bio: Nearly didn't make it to Indianapolis — rushed to the hospital via ambulance earlier in the week after an allergic reaction to salmon coated with cashews. Drove four hours to Indianapolis the next morning. Then delivered 13 points, 10 assists, 4 steals on the biggest stage of his career. The Ringer called it "one of the best 5-for-17 shooting games you'll ever see" — he controlled Michigan's pick-and-roll attack and dissected Arizona's defense. Now has 43 tournament assists, second in Michigan program history. May: "The assistant coaches were like, Elliot's going to have a big game because of the way Arizona defends, but also just because of the stage."

players.name: Trey McKenney
players.meta: G • Freshman • Mr. Basketball of Michigan • Michigan 22-0 when he makes 2+ threes
players.bio: The freshman kept his perfect record intact: Michigan is now 22-0 when McKenney makes multiple three-pointers. Off the bench, Mr. Basketball of Michigan hit 4-of-6 from three for 16 points — looking like, per The Ringer, "one of the best players on the court." The state's top recruit who stayed home keeps delivering in March. McKenney postgame: "We came out, and we really knew how high the stakes were. We just wanted to leave it all on the floor." His four threes helped Michigan extend the lead when Lendeborg was sidelined with the injury.

funFacts.title: Two National Champions, One Floor
funFacts.body: Arizona won the title in 1997 (beating three #1 seeds — the only team ever to do that). Michigan won in 1989. Both are chasing their second championship. The last time two #1 seeds with national championship pedigrees met in the Final Four was Duke vs. Kansas in 2018.

funFacts.title: KenPom #1 vs. KenPom #2
funFacts.body: Michigan's KenPom rating of +39.43 is the second-highest ever recorded, trailing only 1999 Duke. Arizona is KenPom #2. Both teams have +650 scoring differentials — the first time two teams brought that kind of margin to the Final Four since Kansas and Memphis in 2008.

funFacts.title: Cut from His High School Team
funFacts.body: Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg — ESPN's #1-ranked Final Four starter — was cut from his high school team in Puerto Rico for bad grades. He played only 11 total high school games. Then JUCO. Then UAB. Then he turned down $7-9 million from Kentucky to stay at Michigan for this run.

funFacts.title: "Strong Warrior" vs. "April Habits"
funFacts.body: Koa Peat's name means "strong warrior" in Hawaiian. Dusty May has a sign in Michigan's locker room that reads "April Habits" — a lesson from FAU's 2023 Final Four: "We were playing for March habits, and we should have been playing for April." Both teams are built for this moment.

funFacts.title: 8-24 to the Final Four in Two Years
funFacts.body: Michigan went 8-24 in 2023-24 — dead last in the Big Ten. Two years later: 35-3, #1 seed, 95.3 PPG in the tournament, and a Final Four. Dusty May did it through the transfer portal. It may be the greatest turnaround in college basketball history.

funFacts.title: Lithuania vs. Spain in Indianapolis
funFacts.body: Arizona's Motiejus Krivas is 7-2 from Siauliai, Lithuania. Michigan's Aday Mara is 7-3 from Spain. Two international big men, recruited through global pipelines, meeting in the Final Four. Krivas came through Tommy Lloyd's Gonzaga network. Mara transferred from UCLA.

funFacts.title: Back to the Scene of 1997
funFacts.body: The Final Four is at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis — the same city where Lute Olson won Arizona's 1997 national championship at the RCA Dome. Twenty-nine years later, Arizona returns to Indianapolis chasing another title.

funFacts.title: The Fab Five Legacy
funFacts.body: Michigan's most iconic basketball legacy: the Fab Five — Webber, Rose, Howard, King, and Jackson. They reached back-to-back championship games (1992-93) and revolutionized basketball culture. May: "Other than Michael Jordan, I don't think there's ever been a group change the culture of our sport for the better than the Fab Five."

funFacts.title: 90+ in FIVE Straight Tournament Games (NCAA Record)
funFacts.body: Michigan became the first team in NCAA Tournament history to score 90+ in five consecutive tournament games: 101 (Howard), 95 (Saint Louis), 90 (Alabama), 95 (Tennessee), 91 (Arizona). When Roddy Gayle Jr. sank a free throw with 2:34 remaining, it clinched the historic milestone.

funFacts.title: Arizona's Elite Eight Comeback
funFacts.body: Arizona trailed Purdue 38-31 at halftime in the Elite Eight — then outscored them 48-26 in the second half. Purdue went 0-of-8 from three after halftime. Arizona shot 90.9% from the free throw line (20-22). Lloyd: "I wouldn't have said we're going to come out and beat them by 22 in the second half."

funFacts.title: The Coaching Carousel Subplot
funFacts.body: Both coaches have been linked to the open North Carolina job. Lloyd signed an extension through 2031, ending the speculation. May said: "I love it at Michigan, but you'll never hear me comment on any other job unless Michigan lets me go." Fox Sports called it "a distracted Final Four."

funFacts.title: What's a Wolverine, Anyway?
funFacts.body: Michigan is the only school in the country with the wolverine as its mascot. Real wolverines are stocky, muscular carnivores — the largest land-dwelling member of the weasel family — known for taking down prey much larger than themselves. Sound like a team that went from 8-24 to the Final Four?

history.callout: Arizona and Michigan had met just once before in the NCAA Tournament — a 1989 first-round game that Michigan won 91-82 en route to the national championship. Thirty-seven years later, they met again — and the score was eerily similar: Michigan 91, Arizona 73. Michigan's ninth Final Four appearance extended their all-time record to 8-0 in national semifinals. Arizona's drought continues — their first Final Four in 25 years ended in a blowout.

subsections.title: Tournament Paths to the Final Four

timeline.text: Round of 64: Arizona 92, LIU 58 (Burries 18, Peat 15, Kharchenkov 14/10 double-double) | Michigan 101, Howard 80 (Johnson 8-for-8, 21 pts; allowed 14 threes in the first half)
Round of 32: Arizona 78, Utah State 66 (Bradley 18, Krivas 14 reb, Peat 14/10 double-double) | Michigan 95, Saint Louis 72 (Lendeborg 25 pts, 0 TO; all 5 starters in double figures)
Sweet 16: Arizona 109, Arkansas 88 (60 paint pts, held Razorbacks to 21.7% from three) | Michigan 90, Alabama 77 (Lendeborg 23-12-7; bench outscored Bama 33-6)

Elite Eight: Arizona 79, Purdue 64 (Peat 20/7/3, Regional MOP; trailed at half, outscored Purdue 48-26 in 2nd half) | Michigan 95, Tennessee 62 (Lendeborg 27 pts, Regional MOP; 21-0 run broke game open)

Final Four: Michigan 91, Arizona 73. Mara career-high 26 pts (11-16 FG). Lendeborg injured (MCL/ankle) but returned to hit two 3s. Cadeau 13 pts/10 ast double-double. McKenney 16 off bench. Arizona shot 36.6%. Michigan scored 90+ for 5th straight game (NCAA record).

subsections.title: 1989: The Only Previous NCAA Tournament Meeting
subsections.content:
The only time Arizona and Michigan met in the NCAA Tournament was March 16, 1989 — a first-round game in Atlanta. Michigan won 91-82 behind Rumeal Robinson and Glen Rice. The Wolverines went on to win the 1989 national championship — Michigan's only title. Arizona, coached by Lute Olson, was a #5 seed that year and finished 22-10.

Thirty-seven years later, the stakes are considerably higher. Arizona is 36-2 with the #2 KenPom rating. Michigan is 35-3 with the #1 KenPom rating. Both are #1 seeds. Both are coached by men whose careers are defined by what happens next.

subsections.title: Two Programs, Two Eras, One Championship Each
subsections.content:
Arizona, 1997: Lute Olson's Wildcats became the only team ever to beat three No. 1 seeds in a single tournament — Kansas, North Carolina, and Kentucky (84-79 in OT in the championship). Miles Simon was the Final Four MVP. The team had no seniors among its top eight players. Sound familiar? Arizona's 2026 team also relies heavily on freshmen — three freshman starters — and is playing in the same city where that championship was won.

Michigan, 1989: Steve Fisher took over as interim coach six days before the tournament and led Michigan to the title. Glen Rice scored 184 points in the tournament — still an NCAA record. Rumeal Robinson hit two free throws with 3 seconds left to beat Seton Hall 80-79 in the championship. It remains Michigan's only title. Now Dusty May is trying to add the second — and like Fisher, he arrived at Michigan amid upheaval and uncertainty.

footerText: Arizona vs Michigan — Final Four — April 4, 2026 — Lucas Oil Stadium, Indianapolis
